title: HUMORCHAIN: Theory-Guided Multi-Stage Reasoning for Interpretable Multimodal Humor Generation
research_questions: Can explicit humor-theory-guided multi-stage reasoning improve multimodal humorous image captioning over zero-shot, few-shot, rule-based, and CoT prompting baselines?
Does HUMORCHAIN outperform external humorous image captioning systems or caption sources on Oogiri-GO and OxfordTVG-HIC?
How much does a fine-tuned human-preference-guided humor discriminator improve humor detection precision and the final proportion of humorous outputs?
Do the selected four humor theories provide adequate coverage for multimodal humor generation cases?

datasets_used.name: Oogiri-GO
datasets_used.size: 794 pairwise comparisons in I vs G

datasets_used.name: OxfordTVG-HIC
datasets_used.size: 2.9 million image-text pairs with humor ratings and emotional annotations; 1007 pairwise comparisons in I vs H

datasets_used.name: HUMORCHAIN human-annotated humor preference dataset
datasets_used.size: 5,320 image–caption pairs, with 2,511 positive samples and 2,809 negative samples; training set 5,054 samples (2,391 positive, 2,663 negative), validation set 266 samples (120 positive, 146 negative)

methods_proposed.name: HUMORCHAIN
methods_proposed.summary: A theory-guided multi-stage reasoning framework for humorous image captioning. It first generates an image description, judges plausibility, incongruity, and whether humans/animals/cartoons are present, selects one of four generation strategies, applies safety checking, and uses a discriminator-driven retry loop.

methods_proposed.name: Human-preference-guided humor discriminator
methods_proposed.summary: A Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct-based binary classifier fine-tuned with LoRA on human-labeled image–caption pairs, with an added classification head and threshold 0.66. It classifies captions as humorous or non-humorous and triggers rewriting when outputs fail.

humor_theories.detail: Used to detect visual incongruity and guide Absurdity and Emotion_analogy generation, where captions help viewers reinterpret incongruous visual content.
Used especially in Contrast_irony to keep semantic violations within a safe emotional boundary, and supported by compliance filtering for attacks, hate speech, and humiliation.
Used to construct irony, self-deprecation, and Object_analogy captions that map objects or situations to human weakness or everyday failure.
Used in Emotion_analogy and Object_analogy to frame captions as emotional release or tension resolution.

The humor discriminator prompt uses the General Theory of Verbal Humor and asks the model to analyze six Knowledge Resources: Script Opposition, Logical Mechanism, Situation, Target, Narrative Strategy, and Language.
Discussed as part of Attardo and Raskin’s humor-theory lineage and referenced in the discriminator prompt under Script Opposition.
Cognitive Insight Theory and evolutionary psychology are referenced in the discriminator prompt and strategy motivation.

evaluation_methods.detail: Pairwise comparison: annotators saw the same image with two captions and selected the funnier caption, tie, or both not funny; Win Rate, Hard Win Rate, significance tests, Bradley–Terry, and Arena Elo were derived from judgments.
Single-title evaluation: annotators assigned binary humor labels, 1 = humorous and 0 = not humorous, to image–caption pairs.
Humor discriminator dataset annotation: each image–caption pair was independently annotated by five annotators; pairs with at least two humorous labels were treated as humorous.
Generation quality was evaluated with Distinct-1/2, BERTScore / BERT Cross Score, CLIPScore, Embedding Average (EA-Rev), and Greedy Matching (GM-Rev).

Humor detection systems were compared using confusion matrices, positive rate, precision, accuracy-related discussion, acceptance rate, average generations per accepted caption, and precision improvement.

headline_results.finding: HUMORCHAIN won key pairwise internal comparisons, including I vs A, I vs F, and I vs J.
HUMORCHAIN outperformed external comparison groups on Oogiri-GO and OxfordTVG-HIC.
HUMORCHAIN achieved the highest global ranking across human comparisons.
HUMORCHAIN achieved the highest human single-title humor score.
Fine-tuning and the classifier head improved discriminator precision over the untuned baseline.
Adding the discriminator increased the proportion of humorous outputs while adding retry cost.
headline_results.metric: Proportion of Humorous Outputs / Acceptance Rate / Avg. Generations per Accepted Caption / Precision Improvement △
headline_results.value: 45.1% before; 67.0% after; Acceptance Rate 36.5%; Avg. Generations per Accepted Caption 2.74×; Precision Improvement △ +22%

key_findings: Single-strategy prompting changes such as zero-shot, few-shot, rule-based prompting, and CoT produced limited improvements compared with HUMORCHAIN’s explicit multi-stage reasoning.
The ablated theory-guided multi-stage model J also beat A, F, G, and H in pairwise comparisons, supporting the contribution of humor-theory-guided reasoning even without the discriminator.
The discriminator and retry loop improved HUMORCHAIN over J in the direct I vs J comparison, with a win rate of 0.745 for I versus 0.255 for J.
Closed-source LLMs overestimated humor in detection, with high positive rates of 96.2, 87.9, and 94.7 but lower precision than the fine-tuned Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct classifier.
The supplementary theory validation found the four adopted theories applied to 86% of 100 OxfordTVG-HIC samples, with 72% having a single dominant theory.
The main reported failure modes were inadequate image descriptions and non-incongruous images that lacked direct semantic-humor links.
limitations: Theoretical coverage is incomplete because more recent humor frameworks and multidisciplinary perspectives are not systematically integrated.
Culturally embedded irony and reference-dependent implicit jokes may be underrepresented or insufficiently captured.
Humor subjectivity remains a core limitation, and the current annotation/discriminator setup favors majority-preference captions rather than fine-grained individual preferences.
The multi-stage architecture can propagate early misjudgments to later stages, although the paper argues that the discriminator retry loop mitigates this in practice.
Failure cases arise when image descriptions miss contextual cues or emotional nuances needed for humor construction.
Theory-guided Chain-of-Thought reasoning cannot exhaustively cover all humor phenomena and creates a tradeoff between theory coverage and computational complexity.
safety_ethics_notes: HUMORCHAIN includes a compliance detection module that filters group attacks, personal attacks, hate speech, and humiliating or demeaning expressions, including metaphorical references; detected violations trigger automatic rewriting. The paper also notes bias toward majority humor preferences and the need for personalization.

human_samples.population: Volunteer annotators/evaluators for pairwise humorous image caption comparisons; demographic details not reported.
human_samples.observations: Pairwise comparison totals reported as observations, not participant counts: A vs B 300; A vs C 300; B vs D 300; C vs E 300; D vs F 300; E vs F 300; J vs A 300; J vs F 300; J vs G 300; J vs H 300; I vs A 300; I vs F 300; I vs G 794; I vs H 1007; I vs J 300. Response options were A funnier, B funnier, TIE, or Both Not Funny.
human_samples.detail: Appendix H calls participants volunteers and instructs them to judge independently. Number of unique volunteers, items per volunteer, assignment procedure, and demographics are not reported. Caption language is English; annotator language background is not reported.

human_samples.population: Volunteer annotators/evaluators for single-title binary humor evaluation; demographic details not reported.
human_samples.observations: Annotators saw an image and one caption and assigned 1 = humorous or 0 = not humorous. Reported Humor Mean scores include A 0.412, B 0.418, C 0.362, D 0.394, E 0.382, F 0.398, G 0.195, and I 0.810.
human_samples.detail: The paper does not report the number of unique annotators, number of ratings/items in the single-title evaluation, or whether annotators overlapped with the pairwise task.

human_samples.participant_count: 5 annotators per image–caption pair; total unique annotators not reported
human_samples.population: Annotators for the human-preference-guided humor discriminator dataset.
human_samples.observations: 5,320 image–caption pairs were annotated; 2,511 were labeled positive and 2,809 negative. Training set: 5,054 samples, with 2,391 positive and 2,663 negative. Validation set: 266 samples, with 120 positive and 146 negative. A pair was labeled humorous if at least two annotators labeled it humorous.
human_samples.detail: Each pair was independently annotated by five annotators following guidelines. The paper does not report the annotator pool size, demographics, recruitment, or agreement statistics.

judges.name: volunteer annotators
judges.detail: Primary judges for pairwise comparison results, Win Rate, Hard Win Rate, significance tests, Bradley–Terry, and Arena Elo. The paper says captions were labeled A and B, but does not explicitly report blinding to method identity, randomization, or ordering.

judges.name: volunteer annotators
judges.detail: Primary judges for single-title binary humor labels used to compute human Humor Mean scores.

judges.name: five annotators per image–caption pair
judges.detail: Produced human labels for the 5,320-pair discriminator dataset and its 266-sample validation set; labels were aggregated using the at-least-two-of-five humorous threshold.

judges.name: Distinct-1/2; BERTScore/BERT Cross Score; CLIPScore; Embedding Average; Greedy Matching
judges.detail: Automatic metrics used in single-title evaluation for lexical diversity, semantic similarity/creativity, visual–semantic alignment, and sentence/word-level relevance.

judges.name: confusion matrices, precision, positive rate, acceptance rate, average generations per accepted caption
judges.detail: Used to evaluate humor discriminator variants and closed-source LLM humor detection systems against the human-labeled validation set.

judges.name: Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct LoRA + classifier head humor discriminator
judges.detail: LLM-based discriminator judged generated image–caption pairs as humorous or non-humorous, triggered rewriting, accepted positive predictions, and supplied the top-rated fallback candidate after the retry limit. It also produced reported detector predictions in Table 8.

judges.name: GPT-5-2025-08-07
judges.detail: Within HUMORCHAIN, GPT-5 generated image descriptions, plausibility/incongruity/has-human-or-animal-or-cartoon judgments, selected generation pathways, generated captions, and ran the safety classifier for compliance judgments.

judges.name: Gemini-2.5-Flash; GPT-4.1; Claude-3.5-Haiku-20241022
judges.detail: Closed-source LLMs evaluated as 0/1 humor detection systems with structured outputs on the discriminator validation set.

Supplementary theory-coverage validation is reported as 'we validate' on 100 OxfordTVG-HIC samples and statistical analysis of 25% of generated captions; no independent annotators or procedure are specified.

generation_selection.scope: Internal prompt baselines A–F on Meme-Image-No-Text
generation_selection.n: one final caption/title per image per method; no multiple-candidate or best-of-N selection reported
generation_selection.detail: Groups A–F vary zero-shot, few-shot, rule-based/theory prompts, and CoT. The paper reports generated captions for evaluation but does not report candidate pools or selection beyond producing the method output.

generation_selection.scope: Method J: Theory-Guided Multi-Stage Reasoning without discriminator
generation_selection.n: one final caption/title per image; discriminator and retry loop ablated
generation_selection.detail: J retains cognitive humor theory and staged reasoning but excludes the humor discriminator and retry loop.

generation_selection.scope: Method I / HUMORCHAIN with discriminator feedback
generation_selection.n: up to a 5-retry limit; average generations per accepted caption 2.74×; acceptance rate 36.5%
generation_selection.detail: Generated captions classified as non-humorous by the fine-tuned Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct discriminator trigger rewriting. Failed cases after the retry limit default to the discriminator’s top-rated candidate.

generation_selection.scope: External CLoT comparison on Oogiri-GO / Group G
generation_selection.detail: The paper states that captions for randomly sampled Oogiri-GO images were generated separately by HUMORCHAIN and CLoT, but does not report CLoT decoding, candidate count, or selection procedure.

The paper reports generating new HUMORCHAIN captions for randomly sampled OxfordTVG-HIC images and comparing them against existing humorous captions from OxfordTVG-HIC; Group H is not described as newly generated by the authors.

generation_selection.scope: Candidate caption creation for the discriminator annotation dataset
generation_selection.n: multiple candidate captions generated; number per image not reported
generation_selection.detail: The authors generated multiple candidate captions using the HUMORCHAIN/theory-guided framework and randomly sampled image–title pairs for human annotation. The candidate-generation and filtering details before annotation are not fully specified.
Supplement reports n=100 and that J outperforms A with a 0.74 win rate on Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B-Instruct, but does not report candidate counts, decoding settings, or selection.

model_provenance.name: GPT-5
model_provenance.detail: Backbone reasoning model used in the main experiments for HUMORCHAIN and internal baselines/ablations; token pricing and stage parameters are reported, but API/access route and inference date are not.

model_provenance.name: Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct baseline
model_provenance.detail: Un-tuned baseline humor discriminator variant evaluated on the validation set; precision 0.523.

model_provenance.name: Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct + LoRA binary classifier
model_provenance.detail: Fine-tuned discriminator variant evaluated on the validation set; precision 0.636.

model_provenance.name: Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct + LoRA + classifier head
model_provenance.detail: Fine-tuned humor discriminator used in the HUMORCHAIN retry loop and Table 8 detection comparison; precision 0.670.

model_provenance.name: Gemini-2.5-Flash
model_provenance.detail: Closed-source LLM evaluated for humor detection with recommended parameters; no API snapshot or inference date reported.

model_provenance.name: GPT-4.1
model_provenance.detail: Closed-source LLM evaluated for humor detection; the paper also writes GPT-4-1 in prose. No snapshot or inference date reported.

model_provenance.name: Claude-3.5-Haiku
model_provenance.detail: Closed-source LLM evaluated for humor detection; inference date and access route not reported.

model_provenance.name: CLoT (SYSU)
model_provenance.detail: External CLoT model used as Group G for comparison on Oogiri-GO; implementation/checkpoint, decoding, and access details are not reported.

model_provenance.name: Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B-Instruct
model_provenance.detail: Used only in a supplementary backbone-agnostic robustness experiment with n=100; inference settings not reported.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: Stages described: image description, strategy judgment, one selected generation strategy, and safety classifier; exact call count per item including rewrites is not fully reported.
inference_budgets.token_budget: max_tokens = 4000 for Image Description, Strategy Judgment, Object Analogy, Absurdity, Contrast Irony, Emotion Analogy, and Safety Classifier.
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Image Description temperature 0.2; Strategy Judgment temperature 0.1; Object Analogy temperature 0.9; Absurdity temperature 0.8; Contrast Irony temperature 0.9; Emotion Analogy temperature 0.85; Safety Classifier temperature 0.1.
inference_budgets.detail: Parameters are reported in Appendix K.1 for the theory-guided multi-stage reasoning framework.

inference_budgets.scope: Average token consumption and dollar cost for caption generation methods without discriminator, n=100
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: Average cost: A $0.0007; B $0.0008; C $0.0008; D $0.0009; E $0.0073; F $0.0074; HUMORCHAIN without discriminator $0.0082.
inference_budgets.detail: Table 10 states costs are calculated using official token pricing of the backbone model GPT-5-2025-08-07, with 100 samples. The discriminator module is excluded.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: One discriminator decision per generated candidate is implied by the generate–evaluate–refine loop; exact total calls per item are not reported.
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: Up to 5 retries; failed cases default to the discriminator’s top-rated candidate; average generations per accepted caption 2.74×; acceptance rate 36.5%.
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: greedy: false; seed: 3407; top_p: 0.8; top_k: 20; temperature: 0.7; repetition_penalty: 1.0; presence_penalty: 1.5; out_seq_length: 32768.
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: No dollar cost reported for the discriminator or retry loop; retry cost summarized as 2.74× average generations per accepted caption.
inference_budgets.detail: Appendices K.2 and Section 3.2.3 provide discriminator sampling parameters and retry/fallback policy.

inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: The paper states that Gemini-2.5-Flash, GPT-4.1, and Claude-3.5-Haiku-20241022 were evaluated using recommended parameters, but exact values are not reported.
inference_budgets.detail: Table 8 confusion matrices sum to 266 validation examples for each model. Models used unified 0/1 binary classification with structured outputs.
Supplement reports n=100 and a J-vs-A win rate of 0.74, but no inference budget or decoding parameters.

human_baselines.description: No human-written captions generated under matched experimental conditions are reported as a baseline. OxfordTVG-HIC Group H uses existing humorous captions, but the paper does not describe them as a human baseline or specify authorship/creation conditions.
human_baselines.detail: Human annotators evaluate model/caption-source outputs, but human captioner performance under the same task setup is not measured.

human_baselines.description: Human annotations define the gold labels for validation, but the paper does not report a human detector accuracy/precision baseline under matched conditions.
human_baselines.detail: The five-annotator labels are a reference standard, not a reported human-performance baseline.

mitigations: Paper-stated dataset controls include manual screening of Meme-Image-No-Text images for neutrality and reduced cultural ambiguity, but not decontamination.
Paper reports random sampling from Oogiri-GO and OxfordTVG-HIC for external comparisons.
The discriminator dataset uses newly collected human labels, though images/captions are sourced/generated from the aforementioned datasets.
contamination_risk.basis: Oogiri-GO and OxfordTVG-HIC are cited public datasets from 2024 and 2023, evaluated with a later GPT-5 snapshot named GPT-5-2025-08-07 and other models. The paper does not report training-data decontamination, benchmark leakage checks, or whether the primary Meme-Image-No-Text dataset was private/newly created. Random sampling and manual screening address evaluation consistency, not model contamination. Therefore public-benchmark contamination risk remains at least moderate.

llm_judge_dependence.affected_claims: Claims about the discriminator increasing the proportion of humorous outputs, reducing non-humorous outputs, and improving HUMORCHAIN via retry/fallback depend on an LLM-based Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct discriminator. HUMORCHAIN’s delivered outputs are selected or filtered by that LLM judge.
llm_judge_dependence.human_validation: The discriminator is trained and evaluated against five-annotator human labels; Method I vs J is also evaluated with human pairwise comparison over 300 observations, and main generation performance is assessed by human pairwise and single-title evaluations.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: The headline comparative generation results are not primarily based on LLM-as-judge scoring; they rely on human preference judgments plus automatic metrics. However, the method itself uses an LLM judge for output acceptance/selection, and important discriminator/pipeline claims depend on that LLM classifier.

reporting_gaps: Number of unique volunteers/annotators for pairwise and single-title evaluation is not reported.
Annotator demographics, recruitment method, geography, compensation, cultural background, and language background are not reported.
The paper does not report whether pairwise/single-title annotators were blinded to model identity, whether caption order was randomized, or whether task assignment was counterbalanced.
The number of items/ratings in single-title evaluation is not reported separately from the reported Humor Mean scores.
Inter-annotator agreement statistics are not reported for pairwise evaluation, single-title evaluation, or the five-annotator discriminator dataset.
The paper mentions significance tests for pairwise comparisons but does not provide test type, p-values, confidence intervals, or multiple-comparison handling in the supplied tables.
Meme-Image-No-Text dataset size, source provenance, release status, and construction details are underspecified beyond manual screening for neutrality/cultural ambiguity.
Exact image sample counts are partly inferable from pairwise totals but the paper does not consistently distinguish images, pairs, judgments, and annotator votes.
CLoT implementation/checkpoint, inference parameters, candidate counts, and access route are not reported.
OxfordTVG-HIC Group H caption authorship and selection procedure are not described sufficiently to determine whether it is a human baseline.
API/access route, inference date, and provider snapshot details are not reported for GPT-5, Gemini, GPT-4.1, Claude, Qwen, or CLoT beyond model names/snapshots listed in the paper.

Generation parameters for internal baselines A–F and for closed-source LLM humor-detection comparisons are not fully specified; closed-source models are said to use recommended parameters without values.
Candidate counts for most generation methods are not reported; HUMORCHAIN reports a 5-retry limit and average generations per accepted caption but not the full retry distribution.
Training hardware, training time, inference latency, and full compute cost for LoRA fine-tuning and discriminator inference are not reported.
No contamination or decontamination analysis is reported for public legacy benchmarks used with later proprietary models.
Code, data URLs, and artifact availability are not reported in the supplied paper.
